root_motivation: The disk at 97% is accelerating toward full because the two largest target dirs (recall 13G, brain 13G) are LIVE and ballast-guard structurally cannot touch them. careen-guard is the SLO arm that covers exactly what ballast cannot: live target dirs that need selective sweep rather than whole-dir reap. It must compose with ballast-guard via the same event schema so there is one coherent disk-defense story instead of two competing tools with divergent telemetry.
user_persona: Joe Yen running wintermute on an Arch laptop where df / = 97%. ballast-pilot fires hourly but cannot reclaim the 26G in live target dirs. careen-guard runs as the complementary arm, invoked by the same monitoring story.

acceptance_criteria.description: `careen-guard run --mount <fixture>` below the advisory threshold emits a single `Ok` JSON event and runs no sweep.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac1.rs: inject mock disk below advisory_pct; assert event.level == Ok and zero careen-sweep invocations.

acceptance_criteria.description: In the advisory band, it emits a `Warn` event carrying a non-zero `reclaimable_bytes` estimate and performs zero deletions.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac2.rs: inject mock disk in advisory band with mocked careen-survey returning non-zero estimate; assert Warn event with reclaimable_bytes > 0 and no sweep.

acceptance_criteria.description: On a simulated breach, it selects live target dirs in descending-size order, invokes careen-sweep against them, and emits `Breach` with `bytes_reclaimed > 0` and the swept paths in `candidates`.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac3.rs: inject breach-level disk; mock careen-survey and careen-sweep; assert Breach event, bytes_reclaimed > 0, candidates contains swept paths in descending-size order.

acceptance_criteria.description: When safe candidates are exhausted but usage is still above high-water, it emits `BreachUnresolved` (not a panic, not a silent exit).
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac4.rs: inject breach-level disk with mock returning only a small candidate that cannot bring usage below high-water; assert BreachUnresolved event.

acceptance_criteria.description: Schema compatibility: a careen-guard event JSON is successfully deserialized by ballast-guard's `Event` type (or an equivalent test proving field-for-field serde parity).
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac5.rs: emit a careen-guard Event JSON for each level; parse with serde_json into ballast_guard::event::Event (or a local replica of the struct); assert all fields round-trip correctly.

acceptance_criteria.description: careen-guard never selects a dir whose binary is stale/uninstalled (ballast's territory) — asserted by a fixture mixing a ballast-eligible dir and a careen-eligible dir; only the latter is touched.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac6.rs: mock careen-survey returning one stale-uninstalled dir and one live dir; assert only the live dir appears in sweep candidates.

acceptance_criteria.description: `--event-sink` appends one JSON line per pass to the named file in addition to stdout, matching ballast-guard's sink behavior.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac7.rs: run a guard pass with --event-sink set to a tempfile; assert the file has exactly one JSON line that parses as a valid Event.

acceptance_criteria.description: A breach pass that calls careen-sweep respects sweep's lock-safety: if a selected target is build-locked, it is skipped (not corrupted) and the pass continues to the next candidate.
acceptance_criteria.test: tests/acceptance_ac8.rs: mock careen-sweep returning lock-busy on first candidate, success on second; assert second candidate swept, first skipped, event shows reclaimed bytes from second only.

scope: Read disk usage via statvfs with BG_MOCK_DISK_* env var injection for tests
Three-band SLO evaluation: Ok / Warn / Breach / BreachUnresolved
Invoke careen-survey (subprocess) to estimate reclaimable bytes in advisory band
Invoke careen-survey (subprocess) to enumerate live target dir candidates on breach
Invoke careen-sweep (subprocess) to sweep candidates in descending-size order
Skip build-locked candidates on sweep failure, continue to next
Emit ballast-guard-compatible Event JSON to stdout + optional --event-sink file
Config via guard.toml (same shape as ballast's Config: high/low/advisory watermarks)
non_goals: shared watermark evaluator with ballast-guard (open question #1, future)
Live binary detection logic (delegated to careen-survey's classification)

additional.event_schema_compat: careen-guard Event JSON must deserialize against ballast_guard::event::Event unchanged
additional.subprocess_only: careen-sweep and careen-survey are invoked as subprocesses, not linked as libraries
additional.mock_injection: BG_MOCK_DISK_TOTAL + BG_MOCK_DISK_FREE env vars for disk; mock careen-survey/sweep via env var path override

five_whys_trace.q: Why do we need careen-guard when ballast-guard already exists?
five_whys_trace.a: ballast-guard skips any target dir whose binary is current/installed. The two heaviest dirs (recall 13G, brain 13G) are always current, so ballast never touches them.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does ballast skip current-binary dirs?
five_whys_trace.a: Its safety model is whole-dir reap — deleting an in-use binary would break the running system. A finer-grained intra-target sweep is a different operation requiring a different tool.

five_whys_trace.q: Why not just run `cargo clean` on those dirs?
five_whys_trace.a: cargo clean deletes everything including the current binary and all build caches, forcing a full cold rebuild (often 10-20 min). careen-sweep only removes safely regenerable artifacts, preserving the binary and hot cache.

five_whys_trace.q: Why must careen-guard emit the same event schema as ballast-guard?
five_whys_trace.a: A split telemetry story means two separate monitoring pipelines, two log formats, two dashboards. Composability requires one canonical `Event` shape so ballast-pilot (or a future careen-pilot) can handle both without forking.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is this shipped as a standalone guard rather than a flag on ballast-guard?
five_whys_trace.a: ballast-guard's design is whole-dir reap with a `SafetyClass` filter. Adding intra-target sweep as a flag would require plumbing careen-survey's four-class inventory into ballast's two-class model, violating its single-responsibility. Separate tools compose better than a multipurpose monolith.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should careen-guard depend on ballast-guard's event module as a library or replicate its serde contract?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Replicate the serde contract locally (same field names, same kebab-case Level enum) and prove compatibility via an AC5 deserialization test. Avoids a circular dependency and keeps careen-guard standalone-deployable.

ambiguities_resolved.question: How are careen-survey and careen-sweep invoked when they are not yet installed?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Same pattern as ballast-guard uses for ballast-survey/ballast-reap: invoke as subprocesses, return a clear 'not on PATH' error. Tests mock them via environment variable path overrides pointing to a test fixture script.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What does 'live' mean for candidate selection?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: A dir is 'live' if careen-survey classifies it as having a current binary. careen-guard trusts careen-survey's classification output; it does not re-implement the check.
